title: Why Your Shoulders Are Up Around Your Ears
researcherTopic: meditation and neuroscience, the illusion of the self
metaDescription: That chronic tension in your shoulders isn't just stress; it's a history. Learn why your body holds onto old trauma and how to begin the process of release.
imageDescription: Golden light streams into a quiet room, illuminating the subtle circuitry of a nervous system made of delicate, glowing threads. The light traces pathways from the brain down the spine and into the shoulders, where the threads begin to loosen and unclench. The overall feeling is one of peaceful, intelligent release and the warmth of understanding.
htmlContent:
The tension in your shoulders isn’t about stress. It’s about history.

The Armor We Don’t Know We’re Wearing

We carry the past in our bodies, not as memories, but as living architecture. That chronic, unyielding tightness in your shoulders, the one that climbs up your neck and clamps down on the base of your skull, is not a simple byproduct of a long day at the computer. It is a form of physiological armor, forged long ago in the heat of moments when the world felt unsafe and the only available protection was to brace for impact. It’s the body’s real and loyal attempt to keep us safe, a sentinel that never received the order to stand down. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen countless individuals who believe they are just “tense people,” without ever questioning the origin or the intelligence of that tension.

The shoulders, in particular, are a primary site for this kind of somatic holding. They are part of the body’s startle and defense sequence ~ they rise to protect the vulnerable neck and head from a perceived blow. For a child living in an environment of unpredictable anger or emotional neglect, that "blow" might not be physical, but the body’s response is the same. Over time, this defensive posture becomes the default setting, a low-grade, continuous state of preparation for a danger that is now only an echo. We walk through our adult lives prepared for a fight that is already over, the tension in our shoulders a constant, silent testament to battles we have long since survived.

Your Body’s Loyal, Outdated Sentinel

To understand why your shoulders are permanently raised as if in a constant, low-grade shrug, we have to look at the elegant, if sometimes outdated, logic of the autonomic nervous system. This is the part of our internal wiring that operates beneath the level of conscious thought, managing everything from our heartbeat to our threat response. When faced with danger, the sympathetic branch of this system floods us with energy to fight or flee. When fighting or fleeing are not possible ~ as is often the case for a child dependent on their caregivers ~ the system can slam on the brakes, leading to a state of tonic immobility. This is not a passive state; it is a high-energy state of paralysis, a full-body clench that traps the immense energy of the survival response within the musculoskeletal system. Your shoulders are caught in the crossfire of this internal command and counter-command.

Unwinding this pattern is not about telling your shoulders to relax. It’s about updating the predictive model of the brain. It’s about providing the nervous system with new experiences - experiences of safety, of connection, of the threat response being allowed to complete itself... It’s a slow, patient process of demonstrating to the body, on its own terms, that it is safe to finally, after all this time, lay the armor down. We can learn more about this process by exploring how the body keeps the score.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link).

The Unspoken Dialogue Between Tension and Awareness

When we bring our awareness to the sensation of tightness in the shoulders, the initial impulse is often to judge it, to name it as “bad” or “wrong,” and to immediately try to force it to release. We stretch, we massage, we command it to relax, all of which treat the tension as an adversary to be defeated. But what if the tension is not an enemy? What if it is a messenger, a loyal part of us that is holding on for a very good reason? A client once described this as feeling like she was at war with her own body, a conflict she was destined to lose. The shift happens when we move from a stance of opposition to one of curiosity. Is it hot, cold, sharp, dull, vibrating, numb? Where does it begin and where does it end? By approaching the sensation with this kind of gentle, investigative presence, we are doing something radical: we are offering the tense part of ourselves the compassionate witnessing it never received. We are not demanding that it change; we are simply acknowledging its presence. This, in itself, is a powerful new input for the nervous system, a signal that the internal environment is becoming safer.

Let that land for a moment.

From Brace to Breath: The Slow Thaw of the Freeze Response

The primary tool for this thawing process is the breath. Not a forced, controlled breath, but a natural, allowing breath that we can follow with our awareness as it moves through the body. When we direct our attention to the breath, we are anchoring ourselves in the present moment, in the simple, plain reality of this inhalation and this exhalation. As we rest in this anchor, we might gently invite the breath to move into the areas of tension. We don’t send it there; we create an invitation. Often, as the body registers this new quality of attention, small, involuntary movements may arise ~ a twitch, a tremor, a subtle urge to stretch or shift. These are not random; they are the first signs that the trapped survival energy is beginning to discharge. Following these subtle impulses, allowing them to complete themselves without judgment, is a crucial part of how the body unwinds itself from the inside out. It’s a process that cannot be rushed, a delicate negotiation with a part of ourselves that has been holding on for a very long time. For a deeper look at this, one might explore the topic of somatic experiencing.

The Topography of a Body Unburdened

What does it feel like to inhabit a body that is not perpetually braced for impact? For many who have lived inside the armor of complex trauma, this question can feel abstract, even fantastical. The absence of tension is not a neutral state; it is a significantly different way of experiencing the world. The breath deepens, moving from the shallow upper chest into the belly, sending a powerful signal of safety and calm to the entire nervous system. The senses sharpen ~ colors may seem brighter, sounds clearer - as the perceptual field is no longer narrowed by the tunnel vision of the threat response. A sense of spaciousness can begin to emerge, both physically and mentally, a feeling that there is more room inside for all of our experiences, both pleasant and unpleasant.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link).

This path is supported by consistent, gentle engagement with somatic practices. It could be as simple as taking three minutes every hour to bring your awareness to your feet on the floor and the weight of your body in your chair. It could be a more formal practice of yoga, qigong, or a trauma-informed movement modality. As we do this, we are not just releasing muscle tension; we are at its core rewiring our relationship to ourselves, moving from a state of internal fragmentation and conflict to one of greater integration and wholeness. We are learning, at the most striking level, that we can be a safe home for ourselves. For those interested in the mechanisms of this, the work of the National Institute of Mental Health provides extensive research on the neuroscience of trauma and recovery.

Conclusion: The Geography of Return

The work of releasing the armor in our shoulders is not a project of self-improvement, but an act of genuine self-reclamation. It is the slow, patient work of returning to the body, not as a problem to be solved, but as a wise and loyal companion that has been waiting for our attention. By learning to read its story with compassion and awareness, we do not erase the past, but we unburden the present from its weight. We learn to inhabit a body that is not a monument to old wars, but a living, breathing vessel for the life that is here, now.

Enough reading about healing. Enough collecting frameworks and bookmarking articles. At some point the research becomes another form of avoidance. Your body knows this. The only question left is whether today is the day you stop preparing and start practicing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do my shoulders get tense even when I’m not stressed?

Chronic shoulder tension is often not a reaction to present-day stress, but a deeply ingrained pattern from past experiences where your body learned to brace for impact. This defensive posture becomes the nervous system's default setting, a form of armor that remains long after the original threat is gone. It’s your body’s loyal, but outdated, attempt to keep you safe based on old information.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link).

Bear with me on this one.

Can I just stretch the tension away?

While stretching can provide temporary relief, it often doesn’t address the root cause of the tension, which is a nervous system stuck in a defensive state. Lasting change comes from working with your nervous system to update its predictive model. This involves practices that provide new experiences of safety, allowing the body to learn, on its own terms, that it’s safe to release the armor.

What is the first step to releasing this tension?

The first step is not to fight the tension, but to meet it with gentle, non-judgmental awareness. Instead of trying to force your shoulders to relax, simply bring your attention to the physical sensations of the tightness. This act of compassionate witnessing is a radical new input for your nervous system and begins the process of creating internal safety.

How long does it take to feel a difference?

What emerges is a slow, gradual process of unwinding a lifelong pattern, not a quick fix. Some people notice subtle shifts within weeks of consistent practice, while for others it may take longer. The goal is not a one-time release, but the ongoing cultivation of a new relationship with your body, one based on listening and responsiveness rather than command and control.

For targeted muscle tension release, a popular option is Theragun Mini (paid link).

Nobody said this would be comfortable. Do it anyway.
